text: The surgeon performs peritonoscopy and uses a peritoneal carcinometer index with ICG dye to visually distinguish scar tissue from the peritoneum.
The video shows extensive disease affecting the diaphragm peritoneum, Glisson's capsule, omentum, parietal peritoneum, and pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon begins the procedure by using a single blade of scissors combined with monopolar cautery to incise the lateral peritoneal valve.
The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the rectus sheath and posterior transverse fascia while keeping the fat layer down, ensuring the preservation of the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon removes the parietal peritoneum on the left side, resembling the unfolding of a parachute.
The surgeon reaches the psoas muscle and genitofemoral nerve, which are visible in the surgical field.
The retroperitoneal structures are exposed, and the surgeon uses ICG (Indocyanine Green) guidance to visualize and assess these anatomical areas.
The surgeon excises the targeted tissue, removing the entire structure, and uses an APC laser extensively to achieve hemostasis, ensuring minimal bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the large lymph nodes, identified as part of the Rasper-Lyons trial, from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes sheets of peritoneum connected with the lateral line of Toldt, as visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a total parachute-like peritonectomy, removing the entire parietal peritoneum.
The surgeon addresses the left ovarian pedicle, focusing on its identification and management within the surgical field.
Using ICG Firefly imaging, the surgeon identifies the ureter and employs a robotic bezel sealer to take the ovarian pedicle at a high position.

The surgeon performs nerve-preserving surgery in the Okabayashi area, addressing deposits in the pouch of Douglas. The right lateral pelvic peritoneum is connected to the peritonectomy, with the ureter and retroperitoneal vessels carefully guarded. The peritoneum over the appendix and ileocecal area is also taken.
The surgeon preserves the hypogastric nerve during the procedure, ensuring it remains intact amidst the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon proceeds to the pouch of Douglas to perform Aldrich's procedure, continuing the surgical approach in this anatomical area.
The surgeon performs a total pouch of Douglas ectomy, carefully separating it from the adjacent rectal nodule.
The surgeon displays the pouch of Douglas, which is visibly clear of any disease.
The surgeon exposes the bare area of the rectal fibers, revealing the underlying tissue structure.

The surgeon uses a hot shear to perform a peritonectomy, removing the peritoneum from the pelvic region, including the bladder peritoneum and perijureteral tissue. The psoas muscle vessels and genitofemoral nerve are visible during the procedure.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy with nodules using a SureForm stapler, achieving optimal CC-0 cytoreduction. The specimens are bagged. The procedure transitions to Aldrich's procedure, where the sentinel lymph node is harvested. A Foley catheter is capped, and the bladder is distended to facilitate a bladder peritonectomy, connecting into the vesicovaginal septum.
The surgeon carefully preserves and clears Okabayashi's triangle, Yukuba's triangle, and Latzow's triangle, ensuring these anatomical regions remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon uses sharp scissors to excise the entire mesorectum and the diseased peritoneum of the pouch of Douglas, focusing on the rectal fibers.
The surgeon clamps and cuts the uterine vessels at their origin from the internal iliac artery. On the right side, ICG-guided lymph node clearance is performed.

The surgeon develops two anatomical spaces: the paravesical space and the pararectal space, located between the levator ani muscle, obturator nerve, hypogastric nerve, external iliac vessels, ureter, and internal iliac vessels.
The surgical field shows completed clearance of anatomical structures, with visible absence of tissue in previously dissected areas.
The surgeon adjusts the thirty-degree inverting camera to enhance the view of the surgical field.
The surgeon strips the bladder, revealing the uracus, umbilicus, anterior peritoneal cavity, and bladder peritoneum on the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the entire peritoneal sheath from the bladder, ensuring careful handling of the clamped and distended bladder.
The bladder is exposed and appears bare, with no peritoneal sheath covering it.
The rectal fibers are exposed, revealing the bare tissue surface.
The surgeon performs an early colpotomy, making an incision in the vaginal wall to access the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon isolates and ligates the uterine vessels extra peritoneally, away from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates the ocobio space, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate or expose anatomical structures within this area.

The surgeon passes an endobag through the vagina to retrieve the specimen. Docking is performed in the upper abdomen, and a special instrument called the pedjet is used to suspend the stomach. A total supra-colic omentectomy and omental barsectomy are conducted from the foramen of Winslow, along with a lesser omentectomy to remove diseased tissue.
The surgeon carefully safeguards the stomach and the arch under the pancreatic capsule, ensuring protection of these structures during the procedure.
The surgeon strips the peritoneum completely from the right subdiaphragmatic area, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The heart shears make contact with the muscle tissue.
The muscle visibly retracts or 'jumps back' after manipulation, indicating its preservation and elasticity.
The surgeon navigates to the bare area of the layer, focusing on the exposed anatomical region.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the hidden area of the coronary ligament, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon completes the dissection of retroperitoneal para-aortic nodes up to the level of the renal veins on the vena cava and aorta. A hot shear is used to clip the lymphatic duct cisterna. Specimens are retracted to prevent chylous ascites. The closed technique of intraperitoneal chemotherapy (IPEC) is performed using a dedicated machine, followed by hypertonic saline irrigation of the port site. The incision is closed with a subcuticular suture without a bandage.
